Q1: I tried to keep a consistent routine, usually studying in the evenings after work and on weekend mornings. With 11 online courses, I had to be pretty organized just to keep track of everything. I'd make lists of what needed to be done each week and work through them systematically. Being an auditory learner, I'd often listen to lecture recordings while doing other tasks around my flat, which helped me maximize my limited time.
Q2: I aimed for regularity, but honestly, life got in the way a lot. Some weeks I could stick to my 16 hours pretty evenly spread out, but other weeks everything piled up toward deadlines. Working with limited resources means I sometimes had to prioritize paid work over studying, which threw off my schedule. I'd then have to cram more hours into fewer days to catch up.
Q3: Usually it was to check if new content had been posted or to see upcoming deadlines. I'd also log in when I knew an assignment was due soon and needed to submit it. Sometimes I'd get email notifications about discussion posts, and that would prompt me to go have a look. It wasn't always about deep engagement—often just making sure I wasn't missing anything important.
Q4: After the first few weeks, logging in became automatic, like checking my email in the morning. I'd usually do a quick scan of all my courses while having my tea. It became routine because I was juggling so many courses that I couldn't afford to fall behind. The habit formed out of necessity rather than enthusiasm, if I'm being honest.
Q5: The video lectures were absolutely essential for me because I learn best by listening. I watched over 250 minutes of video content because hearing explanations really helps concepts stick for me. I'd often replay sections multiple times until I understood them. The audio format meant I could also listen while commuting or doing household chores, which was crucial given my time constraints.
Q6: I didn't engage much with the discussion forums—only posted about six times and had minimal peer interactions. It's not that I didn't see value in them; I just didn't have the energy for back-and-forth conversations. Reading through lengthy discussion threads felt like a luxury I couldn't afford when I was already stretched thin. I'd skim them occasionally but rarely contributed.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly helped me understand the material or complete assignments. If I could listen to something and immediately apply it to my coursework, that was ideal. I also valued anything that was efficient—if I could learn something in 20 minutes instead of an hour, that mattered a lot to me. With limited resources and time, I had to be strategic about where I invested my effort.
Q8: Required activities always took priority because I knew they affected my grade. Optional activities felt like nice-to-haves that I rarely got to. There was always a slight guilt about skipping optional content, but I had to be realistic about what I could manage. When you're working with limited resources and juggling multiple courses, optional often means 'not happening.'
Q9: Absolutely, I did a lot of studying offline. I'd download lecture audio files and listen to them on my phone during my commute or while cleaning. I also took handwritten notes while listening, which helped me process information better. Sometimes I'd borrow textbooks from the library rather than buying them, and I'd study from those without the LMS knowing anything about it.
Q10: Many times I'd listen to downloaded lectures repeatedly while doing other things—cooking, walking, even before falling asleep. None of that showed up anywhere. I also had a study group that met in person occasionally, where we'd discuss course material and quiz each other. That peer learning was valuable but completely invisible to the system.
Q11: Yes, frequently. I'd access a video lecture once, download the audio, and then listen to it multiple times over the following days. The LMS would only show that one initial access, but I might have engaged with that content five or six times. It was more practical for me to have materials available offline given my schedule and the fact that I couldn't always rely on having good internet access.
Q12: There were definitely periods when I felt quite sad and disconnected from everything, including my coursework. During those times, even logging in felt like a monumental effort. My motivation was already pretty low, and when sadness crept in, it became harder to see the point of pushing through. The mindfulness exercises helped somewhat, but engagement still dropped during those darker periods.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from work or dealing with financial stress, the LMS became just another demand on my depleted energy. I'd sometimes log in, stare at the screen, and not really absorb anything. Competing responsibilities meant coursework often got squeezed into whatever time was left over, which wasn't always quality time. Fatigue made everything feel harder than it needed to be.
Q14: Yes, there were times I'd have videos playing but my mind was elsewhere, worrying about bills or just feeling too tired to focus. I'd technically be 'watching' content but not really processing it. Sometimes I'd realize I'd been staring at a reading for ten minutes without taking anything in. Those sessions felt hollow, like going through the motions without any real learning happening.
Q15: I postponed discussion posts quite a bit because they required mental energy I didn't always have. Writing something thoughtful for others to read felt more demanding than just absorbing content passively. I also avoided some readings when I was feeling particularly low, even knowing they'd help with assignments. It was easier to delay things that required active participation versus passive listening.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my external motivation system since my internal motivation was pretty low. I'd organize my entire week around what was due and when. Without deadlines, I probably would have drifted even more. They created urgency that cut through the sadness and low energy—I'd push through because I had to, not because I wanted to.
Q17: Feedback did affect me, though sometimes not in helpful ways. Positive feedback gave me a temporary boost, but it faded quickly. When I got lower scores than expected, like on exams, it reinforced feelings of inadequacy. I tried to use feedback constructively for assignments, which is probably why my assignment completion was nearly perfect even when exam scores weren't great.
Q18: The structure helped because it gave me clear expectations and timelines. Having content released in a predictable pattern meant I could plan my limited study time effectively. However, with 11 courses, sometimes the structures overlapped in overwhelming ways. I appreciated courses that were straightforward about what was required versus optional.
Q19: Video content made engagement easier because it suited my learning style. What made it harder was when courses required a lot of reading or synchronous participation—my schedule didn't always allow for that. Discussion requirements felt burdensome given my low energy levels. Courses with flexible deadlines or downloadable content were much more manageable for my situation.
Q20: I think sometimes people are genuinely trying but struggling to produce quality work. They might be researching, reading, watching videos, but when it comes to actually creating something, anxiety or confusion stops them. Or they might be dealing with personal issues that make it hard to follow through. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity.
Q21: That definitely reflects my experience somewhat. I passed my courses despite relatively low LMS engagement because a lot of my learning happened offline. Listening to downloaded lectures repeatedly, studying from borrowed books, discussing with peers in person—none of that shows up in the data. You can learn effectively without being constantly visible in the system.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you log in and what you click on, which is useful for identifying students who've completely disengaged. But they miss the quality of engagement entirely. They can't tell if someone is genuinely learning or just going through the motions. They also miss all offline study, emotional state, and the effort it takes someone to just show up when they're struggling.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks. It's when you're curious, when you think about course content even when you're not studying. For me, true engagement happened in those quiet moments listening to lectures and really trying to grasp concepts. It's internal and personal—something a system can't easily measure.
Q24: The data wouldn't show how much effort it took me to engage at all given my low motivation and periods of sadness. It wouldn't capture the mindfulness practices that helped me get through difficult weeks. It can't see that I'm a 27-year-old managing limited resources while taking 11 courses, or that my nearly perfect assignment completion represents significant personal struggle. The human context behind the numbers is completely invisible.
